critique.summary: HP implemented mandatory 15-minute wait times for customer support calls in certain European countries to encourage use of digital self-solve options, but later removed this policy after negative feedback. The company acknowledged the importance of timely access to live support and prioritized improving customer service experience.
mainPoints: HP enforced mandatory 15-minute hold times for support calls in the UK, France, Germany, Ireland, and Italy.
The wait times aimed to push customers towards digital self-solve options by presenting long wait messages even when call centers were not busy.
HP has since lifted the mandatory wait times and emphasized the importance of timely live support.
Internal dissatisfaction was reported among HP employees regarding the forced wait times.
HP previously marketed strong customer support, including 24/7 access and dedicated support buttons on devices like the Dragonfly Pro laptop.
An analysis from Laptop Mag in 2023 rated HP's phone support relatively highly, though some calls could be long.
The article suggests HP's wait time policy may have been financially motivated to reduce staffing needs.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with reports from reputable sources such as The Register, which is cited for the internal communications about the wait times. HP's own statements confirm the policy and its subsequent removal. The context about HP's support offerings and Laptop Mag's analysis are verifiable and accurate. The article provides a balanced view by including HP's official response and employee perspectives, making the factual content reliable.
considerations: The article does not specify the exact timeline when HP removed the mandatory wait times.
It does not provide direct customer feedback or data on how the wait times impacted customer satisfaction.
There is no detailed exploration of how widespread this practice might be among other companies.
The financial implications for HP regarding staffing or cost savings are speculative and not supported by direct evidence.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion critiques HP's mandatory 15-minute support call wait times, highlighting how it alienates competent customers and pushes them towards competitors. Commenters also note the common industry practice of overstating wait times to encourage digital self-service, often with poor quality alternatives.

comments.author: cjs_ac
comments.text: The danger in assuming that all your customers who request support are the sort of person who couldn't empty water from a boot with instructions written on the heel is that all of your competent customers will seek out your more respectful competitors, leaving you with only those who couldn't empty the boot, thus maximising your support costs.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced insight into the negative feedback loop created by poor support policies, accurately reflecting customer behavior and business impact.

comments.author: john_strinlai
comments.text:
>the wait times aimed to “influence customers to increase their adoption of digital self-solve, as a faster way to address their support question. This involves inserting a message of high call volumes, to expect a delay in connecting to an agent and offering digital self-solve solutions as an alternative.”

>Even if HP’s telephone support center wasn’t busy, callers would reportedly hear: We are experiencing longer waiting times and we apologize for the inconvenience.

i am absolutely positive, without proof of course, that this is an extremely common practice. my isp does the exact same thing with basically the same wording. over the years i have called at all times of the day, all days of the week, across all seasons, and it is always "we are experiencing high call volumes right now. but hey, did you know you can do lots of stuff on the website? go to the website. please use the website".

i almost (not really) respect HP for at least admitting to it, rather than all the companies that i suspect are still doing this in the shadows and will never admit to it.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a credible observation about industry-wide practices, supported by anecdotal evidence, adding valuable context to HP's approach.

comments.author: eviks
comments.text:
> and offering digital self-solve solutions as an alternative

But you don't have those as a real alternative! Yes, you do have some "digital", but it's of the same awful quality as this mandatory 15min rule.
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the quality of HP's digital support options, which aligns with common complaints about self-service tools, though it lacks detailed evidence.

comments.author: fancyfredbot
comments.text:
Someone presumably pitched this idea within HP and other people agreed it was something they should try. I guess probably HP didn't put its best and brightest in charge of call centres but still, isn't that sort of amazing?

I wonder if it's the same people who eventually decided it was a bad idea after all, or whether some other group discovered what was happening and got them to stop.
comments.analysis: The comment speculates on internal decision-making at HP, which is plausible but not verifiable, offering a reasonable but unconfirmed perspective.

comments.author: dsr_
comments.text:
"Best available laptop support" apparently means 18/30 or 12/20.

Pretty sure I would consider those both failing grades.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses dissatisfaction with wait times, which is consistent with the article, though it is more of an opinion than an analysis.

comments.author: jqpabc123
comments.text: Just further cements HP's position as one of the most anti-consumer multi-national companies in existence.
comments.analysis: This comment is highly emotional and lacks supporting evidence, making it more of a broad negative opinion than a constructive critique.
